Polk City, FL 33868

citations.narrative:
The facility did not have the record of acquisition for one red-ruffed lemur and one wolf at the time of the inspection.
Accurate and complete acquisition and disposition records are necessary to allow for medical and legal tracking of the
animals. The facility shall ensure that it makes, keeps and maintains records which fully and correctly disclose the
following information concerning all exhibition animals: name and address of person from whom the animal was acquired,
their USDA license number or Driver's license number/state, the name and address of the disposition of the animal, the
date of acquisition and/or disposition, and the species of the animal. The records shall include any offspring born of any
animal while in his or her possession.
To be corrected by April 24, 2026.

On September 15, 2025, the facility representative discovered a young wolf, “Titan” (approximately 6 months old),
deceased. During the inspection, the facility representative disclosed that this wolf pup was having trouble integrating with
the pack and that he was “skinny, a runt, and the other wolves tended to pick on him”. The facility representative
explained that staff would periodically introduce Titan into the primary wolf yard to allow supervised interaction due to his
history of not getting along with the other animals. She also stated that Titan was brought to the attending veterinarian the
day before she found him deceased and that no necropsy was performed. The attending veterinarian stated the pack
never fully accepted Titan into the pack social structure, but he was not brought to her for an examination the day before
he died.
APHIS officials received a copy of a text message written by the same facility representative that states, “Kira killed him
found him in the yard. I didn’t hear them fight at all since we were up by the house and everything Had to be within 30 to
45 min time span.” The facility representative was asked to provide additional details regarding this text message. She
stated she was upset and couldn’t remember exactly the time frame of events. APHIS inspectors conducted a second
interview with the licensee and facility representative two days after the inspection. The licensee, who was not present the
day Titan died, stated Titan's introduction process was appropriate and minimized any ongoing integration issues.
Following this interview, the licensee sent a timeline of events that stated that they were aware that there was “occasional
social friction with certain individual animals” in the group. This timeline stated that “the introduction process remained
slow, supervised, and monitored.” However, the document acknowledged that Titan was left alone with the group,
unmonitored, "what sounded to be a 'scuffle' " occurred, and Titan was found distressed, subdued, and quiet, and died
later that day.
Failure to ensure that animals housed together are compatible, and effectively monitor introductions, can lead to
aggression, injury, and stress, which can compromise animal health and well-being.
Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be compatible, and animals shall not be housed near animals that
interfere with their health or cause them discomfort.
To be corrected from this day, April 21, 2026, forward.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
There is not an adequate pest control program for the garage building that contains birds and the kitchen. During the
inspection, a rodent was seen running behind one of the bird enclosures. There was also an accumulation of rodent feces
behind the refrigerator and food bins. While the facility does set traps, it is not effective in managing the rodent population.
Pests, such as rodents, can spread disease and contaminate food, surfaces, and animal housing areas. A safe and
effective program for the control of insects, ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests must be established and
maintained so as to promote the health and well-being of the birds and reduce contamination by pests in animal areas.
To be corrected by April 29, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
Additional Inspectors:
Moira Roberts, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
The facility did not have the record of acquisition for one red-ruffed lemur and one wolf at the time of the inspection.  
 
Accurate and complete acquisition and disposition records are necessary to allow for medical and legal tracking of the

animal while in his or her possession. 
To be corrected by April 24, 2026. 
3.133  Critical
   
Separation. 
On September 15, 2025, the facility representative discovered a young wolf, “Titan” (approximately 6 months old),

45 min time span.” The facility representative was asked to provide additional details regarding this text message. She

Following this interview, the licensee sent a timeline of events that stated that they were aware that there was “occasional

Animals housed in the same primary enclosure must be compatible, and animals shall not be housed near animals that 
interfere with their health or cause them discomfort. 
To be corrected from this day, April 21, 2026, forward. 
3.158(d) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
There is not an adequate pest control program for the garage building that contains birds and the kitchen. During the 
inspection, a rodent was seen running behind one of the bird enclosures. There was also an accumulation of rodent feces 
behind the refrigerator and food bins. While the facility does set traps, it is not effective in managing the rodent population.  
 
Pests, such as rodents, can spread disease and contaminate food, surfaces, and animal housing areas. A safe and 
effective program for the control of insects, ectoparasites, and avian and mammalian pests must be established and 
maintained so as to promote the health and well-being of the birds and reduce contamination by pests in animal areas.  
To be corrected by April 29, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives. 
 
Additional Inspectors: 
Moira Roberts, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n
